diagrams.title: Java String Pool Memory Architecture
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing how Java manages string literals in a special memory area called the String Constant Pool (SCP - String Constant Pool). The diagram illustrates the heap memory divided into two sections: regular objects and the String Pool.
elements: Heap Memory block divided into two sections
String Pool section labeled 'String Constant Pool' containing pooled string literals: 'Hello', 'World', 'Java'
Regular Objects section containing String objects created with new keyword
Arrow from String Pool to a variable reference showing how variables point to pooled strings
Arrow from regular heap to another variable reference showing new String() objects
Interned string arrow showing how new String().intern() moves to pool
Labels showing memory addresses and references
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how Java optimizes string memory usage through the string pool mechanism, understanding why string literals share memory and when new String() creates separate objects.

diagrams.title: String Concatenation Process Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart-style diagram showing how the + operator performs string concatenation in Java, including the internal conversion to StringBuilder.
elements: Start node representing string1 + string2 operation
Decision diamond showing if both operands are compile-time constants
Path A: String concatenation at compile time using StringBuilder.append()
Path B: Runtime concatenation using new StringBuilder().append(s1).append(s2).toString()
StringBuilder block showing .append() method calls
toString() method call block converting StringBuilder to String
End block showing resulting concatenated string
Arrow labels showing 's1' and 's2' as inputs
Memory allocation box showing 'Creates new String object in heap'
diagrams.purpose: Students understand that + operator internally uses StringBuilder for concatenation, and why repeated concatenations in loops are inefficient compared to StringBuilder directly.

diagrams.title: toString() Method Conversion Flow
diagrams.description: A diagram showing how different Java objects are converted to strings using the toString() method, including default and custom implementations.
elements: Various input objects: Integer, Custom Object, Array, StringBuilder
toString() method box with 'public String toString()' label
Default implementation showing class name + '@' + hashCode hex
Custom toString() override showing formatted output like 'Person{name=John, age=25}'
System.out.println() statement receiving the string result
Output display showing the string representation
Null handling box showing 'null' output when object is null
String concatenation integration showing obj + " text"
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how any Java object can be converted to string representation, the difference between default and custom toString() implementations, and how this enables debugging and display.

diagrams.title: String Literal vs new String() Comparison
diagrams.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram showing the memory and behavior differences between creating strings using literals versus the new keyword.
elements: Left side labeled 'String Literal': str1 = "Hello" showing variable pointing to String Pool
Right side labeled 'new String()': str2 = new String("Hello") showing variable pointing to heap object
Equality comparison: str1 == str2 showing false (different references)
Equality comparison: str1.equals(str2) showing true (same content)
Memory address labels: str1 at address 0x100 (pool), str2 at address 0x200 (heap)
intern() method arrow showing str2.intern() pointing to pool location
Variable table showing str1 and str2 with their respective memory locations
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the critical difference between reference equality (==) and content equality (equals), and how the string pool affects string comparison in Java.

charts.title: String Concatenation Methods Comparison
charts.content: Three-column comparison table: Method | Performance | Use Case. concat(): Fast for 2-3 strings, direct append, single operation - Best for simple concatenation. + Operator: Slow in loops, creates StringBuilder internally - Best for readability in simple statements. StringBuilder: Fastest for loops/multiple appends, mutable, no extra objects - Best for loops and dynamic building. StringBuffer: Similar to StringBuilder but synchronized, thread-safe - Best for multi-threaded environments.

charts.title: String Creation Decision Flowchart
charts.content: Decision flowchart for choosing string creation method: Start → Do you need to modify the string later? → Yes → Use StringBuilder/StringBuffer. No → Continue → Is this a compile-time constant that will be reused frequently? → Yes → Use string literal "text". No → Continue → Do you explicitly need a new object in heap? → Yes → Use new String("text"). No → Use string literal "text". End → Summary: Use literals for constants, StringBuilder for loops, new String() only when explicit heap object needed.

charts.title: String Pool Behavior Summary
charts.content: Four-row table covering pool operations: Literal creation: "Hello" - Creates reference to pool, no new object. new String(): new String("Hello") - Creates heap object, does not add to pool automatically. intern(): str.intern() - Moves heap string to pool, returns pool reference. == operator: Checks reference equality (memory address). equals(): Checks content equality (character sequence). Use intern() when comparing many strings to save memory.

branches: String Concatenation (+ operator, concat(), StringBuilder, StringBuffer)
String Literals & Pool (SCP, intern(), memory optimization)
toString() Conversion (default, custom override, null handling)
Reference vs Content Equality (== vs equals(), pool effects)
Memory Management (heap vs pool, garbage collection)
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all special string operations, showing how they relate to memory management, performance optimization, and practical string handling in Java applications.
